prompt: Which motherboard form factor is the largest of the common consumer sizes, measuring approximately 12 x 9.6 inches and offering the most expansion slots?
rationale: Standard ATX is roughly 12 x 9.6 inches and is the largest of the common mainstream consumer form factors, giving it the most room for PCIe slots, RAM slots, and connectors.

optionNotes.A: Micro-ATX is smaller (about 9.6 x 9.6 in) with fewer expansion slots.
optionNotes.B: Mini-ITX is a very small 6.7 x 6.7 in board, typically with only one expansion slot.
optionNotes.D: Nano-ITX is even smaller than Mini-ITX (4.7 x 4.7 in), used in embedded/SFF devices.

prompt: What does the acronym POST stand for in the context of a computer's startup process?
rationale: POST stands for Power-On Self-Test, a diagnostic sequence the firmware (BIOS/UEFI) runs at startup to verify core hardware such as CPU, RAM, and the video adapter before handing control to the bootloader.

optionNotes.A: There is no 'Primary Operating System Transfer'; the OS is loaded later by the bootloader, not by POST.
optionNotes.C: POST tests core internal hardware, not just peripheral output.
optionNotes.D: This is a fabricated term unrelated to the firmware startup test.

prompt: Which RAID level mirrors data identically across two drives, providing redundancy but no usable capacity gain?
rationale: RAID 1 is disk mirroring: the same data is written to both drives, so the array survives a single drive failure but usable capacity equals that of one drive.

optionNotes.A: RAID 0 stripes data for performance/capacity with no redundancy; a single drive failure loses everything.
optionNotes.B: RAID 5 uses striping with distributed single parity, not mirroring, and needs at least three drives.
optionNotes.D: RAID 6 uses striping with double distributed parity (needs at least four drives), not mirroring.

prompt: Which connector is the main power connector that supplies power from the PSU to a modern ATX motherboard?
rationale: The 24-pin ATX connector is the primary motherboard power connector on modern ATX systems, delivering the main rails to the board.

optionNotes.B: The 15-pin SATA power connector powers SATA drives, not the motherboard.
optionNotes.C: The 4-pin Molex connector is a legacy peripheral power connector for older drives and fans.
optionNotes.D: The 6-pin PCIe connector supplies supplemental power to graphics cards, not the motherboard itself.

prompt: Which memory module is physically smaller and designed primarily for laptops and small-form-factor systems?
rationale: SO-DIMM (Small Outline DIMM) is the compact memory module used in laptops, mini-PCs, and many all-in-ones because of its reduced length compared to a full-size DIMM.

optionNotes.A: A standard DIMM is the full-length module used in desktops and servers.
optionNotes.C: RIMM is an obsolete Rambus (RDRAM) module, not the standard laptop form factor.
optionNotes.D: SIPP is an obsolete single in-line pin package memory format from early PCs.

prompt: What is the maximum theoretical data transfer rate of the SATA III (SATA 6Gb/s) interface?
rationale: SATA III runs at a 6 Gb/s signaling rate; after 8b/10b encoding overhead the usable throughput is about 600 MB/s, which is why SATA SSDs plateau near that figure.

optionNotes.A: About 150 MB/s is the rate of the original SATA I (1.5 Gb/s).
optionNotes.B: About 300 MB/s corresponds to SATA II (3 Gb/s).
optionNotes.D: About 985 MB/s per lane describes a PCIe 3.0 lane (used by NVMe), not the SATA III bus.

prompt: An NVMe M.2 SSD achieves dramatically higher speeds than a SATA SSD primarily because it communicates over which interface?

options.text: The SATA III bus through the AHCI controller

rationale: NVMe drives connect over PCIe lanes, providing far greater bandwidth and lower latency than the SATA bus. An M.2 slot can carry either SATA or PCIe/NVMe signaling, but it is the PCIe path that gives NVMe its speed.

optionNotes.A: Using the SATA bus with AHCI caps a drive at SATA III's ~600 MB/s; that is exactly what NVMe bypasses.
optionNotes.C: USB is an external peripheral bus, not how internal NVMe drives connect to the system.
optionNotes.D: IDE/PATA is an obsolete parallel interface far slower than even SATA.

prompt: Which statement best describes the purpose of a TPM (Trusted Platform Module) in a modern PC?

options.text: It accelerates 3D graphics rendering for games and CAD applications.
It is a dedicated cryptoprocessor that securely stores keys used for features like disk encryption and Secure Boot.
It increases the number of usable PCIe lanes available to the CPU.
It provides temporary high-speed cache shared between the CPU and GPU.

rationale: A TPM is a hardware cryptoprocessor that generates, stores, and protects cryptographic keys. It underpins capabilities such as BitLocker drive encryption and platform-integrity measurements that support Secure Boot.

optionNotes.A: Graphics rendering is handled by the GPU, not the TPM.
optionNotes.C: PCIe lane allocation is a function of the CPU and chipset, unrelated to the TPM.
optionNotes.D: The TPM is not a performance cache; CPU/GPU caches are separate SRAM structures.

reference: Computer Hardware › BIOS/UEFI, TPM & Secure Boot

prompt: To enable dual-channel memory operation on a typical four-slot motherboard, how should two matched RAM modules normally be installed?

options.text: Both modules in the two slots closest to the CPU, ignoring color coding
In the two slots of the same color (matching channels), per the motherboard manual
One module only, since dual-channel uses a single high-capacity stick
Both modules in slots 1 and 2, which are always the same channel

rationale: Boards interleave two channels across the four slots and usually color-code the matched pairs (e.g., slots A2 and B2). Populating the two same-colored slots as directed by the manual enables dual-channel. Slot order alone does not guarantee correct channel pairing.

optionNotes.A: The two slots closest to the CPU are commonly the same channel, not opposite channels, so this often yields single-channel.
optionNotes.C: Dual-channel requires two modules running in parallel across two channels, not one stick.
optionNotes.D: Slots 1 and 2 are frequently the same color/channel, so this typically does not enable dual-channel.

prompt: Which video interface is purely digital, supports audio over the same cable, and is the most common connector found on consumer TVs and monitors today?
rationale: HDMI carries digital video and audio over a single cable and is the dominant connector on consumer displays and TVs. While DisplayPort and DVI-D are also digital, HDMI is the ubiquitous consumer standard that also natively carries audio.

optionNotes.A: VGA is an analog-only video interface that carries no audio.
optionNotes.B: DVI-D is digital but does not natively carry audio (standard DVI carries no audio).
optionNotes.D: Composite video is a low-resolution analog signal, not a modern digital interface.

prompt: Which statement about ECC memory is correct?

options.text: ECC RAM can detect and correct single-bit memory errors, making it common in servers and workstations.
ECC RAM is faster than non-ECC RAM because it skips parity calculation.
ECC is a cooling technology applied to memory modules.
ECC RAM works only in laptops and is incompatible with desktops.

rationale: ECC (Error-Correcting Code) memory uses extra bits to detect and automatically correct single-bit errors (and detect many multi-bit errors), which is why it is favored where data integrity is critical, such as servers and workstations.

optionNotes.B: ECC adds a small overhead for its check bits, so it is typically slightly slower, not faster, than equivalent non-ECC.
optionNotes.C: ECC is an error-correction scheme, not a cooling technology.
optionNotes.D: ECC is mainly a server/workstation feature requiring supporting CPU and chipset; it is not laptop-only.

prompt: A computer's CPU is described as 64-bit. What is the primary practical advantage this gives over a 32-bit CPU?

options.text: It can address far more than 4 GB of RAM (well beyond the 32-bit limit).
It always doubles the clock speed of the processor.
It eliminates the need for a cooling solution.
It removes the need for a chipset on the motherboard.

rationale: A 32-bit CPU/OS can address only about 4 GB of memory (2^32 bytes). A 64-bit architecture can address vastly more RAM, which is its key practical benefit (along with wider registers).

optionNotes.B: Bit width relates to register size and addressable memory, not clock speed.
optionNotes.C: All modern CPUs still require cooling regardless of bit width.
optionNotes.D: A chipset is still needed; bit width does not change that.

prompt: Which RAID configuration combines mirroring and striping, requires a minimum of four drives, and offers both redundancy and improved performance?
rationale: RAID 10 stripes data across mirrored pairs, so it needs at least four drives and delivers the redundancy of mirroring plus the read/write performance of striping.

optionNotes.A: RAID 5 uses single distributed parity (min three drives), not mirroring + striping.
optionNotes.B: RAID 0 is striping only, with no redundancy.
optionNotes.D: RAID 1 is mirroring only and does not stripe.

prompt: Which power connector is most commonly used to provide supplemental power to the CPU near the top of an ATX motherboard?
rationale: Modern boards use a 4+4 / 8-pin EPS12V connector at the top of the board to feed the CPU's voltage regulator with dedicated 12V power, separate from the 24-pin main connector.

optionNotes.B: The 24-pin connector is the main board power connector, not the dedicated CPU 12V feed.
optionNotes.C: SATA power connectors feed drives, not the CPU VRM.
optionNotes.D: Molex is a legacy peripheral connector, not the CPU power feed on modern boards.

prompt: In the laser printing imaging process, which step uses the laser to write a latent image by selectively neutralizing charge on the photosensitive drum?
rationale: In the seven-step process (processing, charging, exposing, developing, transferring, fusing, cleaning), the exposing/writing step is where the laser strikes the uniformly charged drum to discharge specific areas, forming the latent electrostatic image.

optionNotes.A: Charging applies a uniform charge to the drum before the laser writes; it does not form the image.
optionNotes.C: Developing applies toner to the latent image already written by the laser.
optionNotes.D: Fusing uses heat and pressure to bond toner permanently to the paper at the end.

prompt: A high-end GPU is installed in a PCIe x16 slot, but the system reports it is running at x8. Which is the most likely hardware-level explanation?

options.text: The slot is physically x16 but electrically wired for only 8 lanes, or lanes are shared with another populated slot/M.2 device.
The GPU is defective and must be replaced because x16 cards cannot run at x8.
The power supply lacks an 8-pin connector, which forces the slot into x8 mode.
The motherboard BIOS has disabled the integrated GPU, halving the lanes.

rationale: A physical x16 slot can be electrically wired for fewer lanes (x8 or x4), and on many boards installing a second card or populating certain M.2 slots causes the CPU's limited lanes to be split, dropping the primary slot to x8. This is normal lane allocation, not a fault.

optionNotes.B: x16 cards are designed to operate fine at x8 (with minor performance impact); link width dropping does not mean the card is defective.
optionNotes.C: Missing supplemental power can prevent the card from working or throttle it, but it does not set the PCIe link width to x8.
optionNotes.D: Disabling integrated graphics does not reduce a discrete card's PCIe lane count.

prompt: Which statement most accurately describes Thunderbolt 3 connectivity?

options.text: It uses the USB-C connector, can provide up to 40 Gb/s of bandwidth, and can carry PCIe and DisplayPort signals.
It uses a proprietary Mini DisplayPort connector and is limited to 10 Gb/s.
It is purely a video standard and cannot carry data or power.
It is electrically identical to USB 2.0 and caps out at 480 Mb/s.

rationale: Thunderbolt 3 adopted the USB-C connector, delivers up to 40 Gb/s, and can tunnel PCIe and DisplayPort over the same cable, in addition to supplying power. This versatility is its defining trait.

optionNotes.B: The Mini DisplayPort connector and lower speeds describe earlier Thunderbolt 1/2, not Thunderbolt 3.
optionNotes.C: Thunderbolt carries data, video, and power, not video alone.
optionNotes.D: 480 Mb/s is USB 2.0; Thunderbolt 3 is far faster at up to 40 Gb/s.

prompt: A technician must explain why an inkjet printer differs fundamentally from an impact (dot-matrix) printer. Which comparison is correct?

options.text: Inkjet sprays liquid ink droplets onto paper, while an impact printer strikes an inked ribbon against the paper to form characters.
Inkjet uses heat to fuse toner powder, while impact printers spray ink.
Both use a photosensitive drum and laser to create the image.
Impact printers are the only type that can print multipart (carbon-copy) forms because they spray ink through nozzles.

rationale: Inkjet printers eject tiny liquid ink droplets through nozzles onto the page, whereas impact (dot-matrix) printers physically strike pins against an inked ribbon and the paper. The mechanical striking action is the defining difference.

optionNotes.B: Fusing toner powder describes a laser printer, not an inkjet; impact printers strike a ribbon, they do not spray ink.
optionNotes.C: Drums and lasers describe laser printers, not inkjet or impact printers.
optionNotes.D: Impact printers can indeed print multipart carbon forms, but precisely because they strike the paper, not because they 'spray ink through nozzles' (that statement is self-contradictory).
